project_and_lead_agency_information.lead_agency: City of Riverbend Community Development Department

project_location.within_2_miles_schools: Riverbend High School; Oak Meadow Elementary School; Riverbend Community College satellite campus

development_type.transportation_type: Internal public streets, roundabouts, Class II bike lanes, and multi-use trails connecting to existing city network
development_type.educational: A small private early childhood education center (approximately 6 classrooms, 8,000 sq ft) intended to serve residents of the project and surrounding neighborhoods, with capacity for approximately 72 children ages 2–5 and 14 staff members.
development_type.recreational: Neighborhood park (4.5 acres) with playgrounds, picnic areas, community garden plots, and a small amphitheater; linear greenbelt with walking/jogging paths; two pocket parks with fitness stations; and a dog park integrated into the greenbelt system.
development_type.water_facilities_type: On-site potable water distribution lines, recycled water irrigation lines, and a new 12-inch off-site connection to the City’s existing water main in E 20th Street (no new treatment plant).
development_type.other_development_type: Live-work units (ground-floor flex space with residential above) along the main street spine, totaling approximately 20,000 sq ft of flexible commercial/creative space integrated with 36 of the residential units.

project_issues_discussed_in_document.other_project_issues: Energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions; environmental justice and equitable access to housing and services; construction-related vibration impacts on nearby residences; and potential short-term business disruption along E 20th Street during installation of utility connections.

land_use_and_project_description.present_land_use_zoning_general_plan_des:
The approximately 48.7-acre project site consists of three contiguous parcels located within the southern urbanized area of the City of Riverbend. The site is currently designated in the City’s General Plan as “Community Commercial” (CC) along the E 20th Street frontage (approximately 10 acres) and “Medium Density Residential” (MDR, 8–14 du/ac) on the remaining interior acreage. Zoning designations are split between C-2 (Community Commercial) along the arterial corridor and R-2 (Medium Density Residential) on the interior parcels. Existing uses include a fallow former walnut orchard with scattered remnant trees, a decommissioned fruit packing shed and associated outbuildings, and two single-story residential structures that are currently vacant and boarded. Surrounding land uses include single-family residential neighborhoods to the north, a regional shopping center and associated surface parking to the west, a mix of light commercial and multi-family residential to the east along E 20th Street, and a riparian corridor and open space associated with Little Chico Creek to the south. The site is largely disturbed, with compacted soils from prior agricultural and light industrial activities, and is served by existing paved access from E 20th Street and an unimproved gravel drive from Riverbend Parkway.

The project applicant is requesting a General Plan Amendment to redesignate portions of the site to “Mixed Use Neighborhood” (MUN) and “Parks/Open Space,” a Rezone to a new Specific Plan / Planned Unit Development overlay zone, and approval of the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Specific Plan, Vesting Tentative Subdivision Map, Planned Unit Development permit, and associated Site Plan and Use Permit entitlements.
land_use_and_project_description.project_description:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is a comprehensive infill development proposed on approximately 48.7 acres within the City of Riverbend, Butte County, California. The project is intended to transform an underutilized, partially vacant former agricultural and light industrial property into a walkable, transit-supportive mixed-use neighborhood that provides a diversity of housing types, neighborhood-serving commercial uses, and integrated parks and open space.

Key components of the proposed project include:

1. Residential Development (312 units total)
- Approximately 24.3 acres of the site would be developed with a mix of residential product types, including:
  • 96 single-family detached small-lot homes (approximately 3,000–4,000 sq ft lots), generally located along the northern and eastern portions of the site.
  • 120 townhome units arranged in 3- and 4-plex configurations, with shared motor courts and small private patios, located primarily in the central portion of the site and along the main street spine.
  • 60 stacked flats in three-story buildings over tuck-under parking, oriented around the neighborhood park and greenbelt.
  • 36 live-work units with ground-floor flex/office/creative studio space (approximately 500–700 sq ft per ground-floor space) and two-story residential units above. These units are located along the project’s main street to create an active frontage.
- Overall residential density would average approximately 12.8 units per net residential acre, consistent with the proposed “Mixed Use Neighborhood” General Plan designation.

2. Commercial and Office Uses
- Approximately 64,000 sq ft of neighborhood-serving commercial uses would be developed along the E 20th Street frontage and at the primary internal intersection. Anticipated uses include a small-format grocery store (approximately 25,000 sq ft), cafes and restaurants, personal services (e.g., salon, dry cleaner, fitness studio), and small professional offices.
- An additional 85,000 sq ft of office space would be provided in two- and three-story multi-tenant office buildings located along the western portion of the site adjacent to the existing regional shopping center. These buildings are anticipated to accommodate professional services such as medical offices, financial services, small tech firms, and co-working space. Combined, the commercial and office components are projected to support approximately 490 jobs at full buildout.

3. Parks, Open Space, and Recreation
- A centrally located 4.5-acre neighborhood park would include multi-purpose turf fields, a children’s playground with inclusive and accessible play equipment, picnic areas with shade structures and barbeque facilities, a small amphitheater with terraced seating for community events, and restroom and storage buildings.
- A linear greenbelt and multi-use trail system would extend along the southern edge of the site, providing a landscaped buffer to the Little Chico Creek riparian corridor. The greenbelt would include a 12-foot-wide multi-use path for pedestrians and cyclists, fitness stations, seating nodes, and interpretive signage about the local ecosystem and the site’s agricultural history.
- Two pocket parks (approximately 0.5 acre each) would be integrated into residential neighborhoods, providing smaller-scale play areas and passive open space.
- A fenced off-leash dog park area (approximately 0.75 acre) would be incorporated into the greenbelt, with separate small and large dog areas, shade trees, and seating.

4. Early Childhood Education Center
- The project includes an approximately 8,000 sq ft private early childhood education center near the neighborhood park. This facility would operate as a preschool and childcare center serving both project residents and the broader community, with hours generally from 7:00 a.m. to 6:00 p.m. on weekdays. The center would include six classrooms, an outdoor play yard, staff offices, and support spaces.

5. Circulation, Access, and Transportation Improvements
- Primary vehicular access would be provided via two new signalized intersections on E 20th Street and one full-access intersection on Riverbend Parkway. Internal circulation would consist of a modified grid of public and private streets designed for low-speed operation.
- The project would construct Class II bike lanes along both sides of the new internal collector street and along the project’s E 20th Street frontage, with connections to existing city bike routes.
- A network of sidewalks, crosswalks, and traffic-calming features (including raised crosswalks, curb extensions, and two single-lane roundabouts) is proposed to enhance pedestrian safety and reduce vehicle speeds.
- The project would include new bus stops with shelters along E 20th Street, coordinated with the Butte Regional Transit system to improve transit access for residents and employees.

6. Utilities and Infrastructure
- Water: The project would connect to the City of Riverbend’s municipal water system via a new 12-inch main extension in E 20th Street and on-site distribution lines. A recycled water line would be extended from an existing main in Riverbend Parkway to serve landscape irrigation demands for parks, parkways, and common areas.
- Wastewater: Sanitary sewer flows would be conveyed via new on-site gravity sewer mains to an existing 15-inch trunk line in Riverbend Parkway, which ultimately discharges to the City’s wastewater treatment plant. No new treatment facility is proposed, but off-site improvements may include upsizing a short segment of the existing trunk line to ensure adequate capacity.
- Stormwater: The project would implement a low-impact development (LID) strategy, including bioswales, bioretention planters, and underground detention facilities to capture and treat runoff prior to discharge to the City’s storm drain system. The stormwater system is designed to maintain or reduce post-project peak flows relative to existing conditions for the 2-, 10-, and 100-year storm events.
- Dry Utilities: New underground electric, natural gas, telecommunications, and cable lines would be extended into the site from existing infrastructure in adjacent streets.

7. Entitlements and Phasing
- Discretionary approvals include: (1) General Plan Amendment; (2) Rezone to a Specific Plan / Planned Unit Development (PUD) overlay; (3) adoption of the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Specific Plan; (4) Vesting Tentative Subdivision Map; (5) Planned Unit Development permit; (6) Site Plan and Architectural Review; (7) Use Permits for the early childhood education center, grocery store with on-site alcohol sales, and drive-through restaurant(s); and (8) Development Agreement.
- Buildout is anticipated to occur over approximately 8–10 years in four major phases, beginning with backbone infrastructure and the initial residential neighborhoods, followed by the neighborhood commercial center, office buildings, and completion of the park and trail system. Phasing is structured to ensure that key community amenities, such as the neighborhood park and portions of the greenbelt trail, are delivered early in the project timeline.

8. Objectives and Community Benefits
- The project is intended to: (a) provide much-needed housing supply in a range of price points and formats, including opportunities for first-time homebuyers and “missing middle” housing; (b) locate new housing and jobs within an existing urbanized area near transit, services, and employment centers, thereby reducing vehicle miles traveled (VMT) relative to more remote greenfield development; (c) create a walkable, mixed-use environment with convenient access to daily needs; (d) enhance connectivity to existing neighborhoods, parks, and regional trails; and (e) remediate and repurpose a partially blighted site with aging structures and underutilized land.

The Draft EIR evaluates the potential environmental impacts associated with construction and operation of the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project, identifies feasible mitigation measures to avoid or substantially lessen significant impacts, and considers a reasonable range of project alternatives, including a reduced-intensity alternative and an office-emphasis alternative.
